coredata.dc:title: Deconvolution of photoluminescence spectra and electronic transition in carbon dots nanoparticles from microcrystalline cellulose
coredata.dc:creator: Balanta, M.A.G.
da Silva Filho, W.J.F.
Souza, M.C.G.
de Assunção, R.M.N.
Champi, A.
Cuevas, R.F.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are nanomaterials with promising applications in several areas such as optoelectronics and biomedicine due to their low toxicity and strong luminescence intensity. Understanding the emission and bandwidth of the fluorescence spectrum is still a challenge in the scientific community due to the dependence of emission on the excitation wavelength. In this work, the photoluminescence properties of carbon dots obtained from microcrystalline cellulose (MCC) were studied. The optical properties were studied through measurements of photoluminescence (PL), photoluminescence excitation (PLE), and absorption. Systematic adjustments were made through Gaussian deconvolution of the PL spectrums and the coupling between electronic states was analyzed through PLE and optical absorption measurements. From the Gaussian adjustments, bandwidth values of ∼25 nm and ∼100 nm were found for the emission associated with the core and the surface states respectively, these values are consistent with the bandwidth of the PLE spectra with tuned detection in these states. As evidenced by the results, there are contributions from the core and surface functional groups in the luminescence emission of CDs. Furthermore, with recent analyzes that attribute the radiative emission of CDs only to the surface trap states, we observed that the core present emission and absorption of light for samples with specific pH and reaction times. These results should contribute to the understanding of the electronic transitions of CDs for applications related to the luminescent optical properties of these nanomaterials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Photoluminescent nanomaterial
Microcrystalline cellulose nanoparticles
Deconvolution of the luminescence spectrum
Core and surface trap states in carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photoluminescence, photoluminescence excitation (PLE), and absorption characteristics of CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the experimental steps to obtain the CDs obtained as a by-product of the production of a solid acid catalyst (SAC) [22].
Representative SEM Images of the sample t:1h_pH:5 and d EDS spectrum. Selected magnification in a region of the sample to visualize the formation of the Carbon dots cluster.
FTIR spectrum for the sample t:1h_pH:5.
Raman spectrum of the Carbon Dots produced with 1 h of reaction time at pH = 0 and pH = 5.
Optical characterization of PL, PLE, and absorbance for samples: (a) t:1h_pH:0 and (b) t:6h_pH:0. Regions between the dashed lines identify the different ranges of absorption by the sample.
Photoluminescence spectrum with variation of excitation wavelength for sample t:1h_pH:0.
Comparison of photoluminescence spectra and Gaussian adjustments of the studied samples. Excitation wavelength ⎣Exct = 310 nm.
The full width at half maximum was obtained from the Gaussian fit, as a function of the excitation wavelength for all samples studied.
Wavelength centered on the emission maximum obtained from the Gaussian adjustment, as a function of the excitation wavelength for all samples studied.
Schematic representation of the emission configuration of the studied CDs. Inset: energy band diagram of the transition channels.
Excitation photoluminescence (PLE) spectra to the sample t:6h_pH:0. Detection has been tuned in the three emissions obtained in the deconvolution of the PL spectrum presented in the inset.

head.ce:title: Deconvolution of photoluminescence spectra and electronic transition in carbon dots nanoparticles from microcrystalline cellulose

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Universidade Federal do ABC, Centro de Ciencias Naturais e Humanas.Santo Andre, SP, Brazil

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are nanomaterials with promising applications in several areas such as optoelectronics and biomedicine due to their low toxicity and strong luminescence intensity. Understanding the emission and bandwidth of the fluorescence spectrum is still a challenge in the scientific community due to the dependence of emission on the excitation wavelength. In this work, the photoluminescence properties of carbon dots obtained from microcrystalline cellulose (MCC) were studied. The optical properties were studied through measurements of photoluminescence (PL), photoluminescence excitation (PLE), and absorption. Systematic adjustments were made through Gaussian deconvolution of the PL spectrums and the coupling between electronic states was analyzed through PLE and optical absorption measurements. From the Gaussian adjustments, bandwidth values of ∼25 nm and ∼100 nm were found for the emission associated with the core and the surface states respectively, these values are consistent with the bandwidth of the PLE spectra with tuned detection in these states. As evidenced by the results, there are contributions from the core and surface functional groups in the luminescence emission of CDs. Furthermore, with recent analyzes that attribute the radiative emission of CDs only to the surface trap states, we observed that the core present emission and absorption of light for samples with specific pH and reaction times. These results should contribute to the understanding of the electronic transitions of CDs for applications related to the luminescent optical properties of these nanomaterials.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Core and surface trap states in carbon dots

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Since its discovery by Xu et al. [1], Carbon Dots (CDs) have attracted considerable attention due to their fluorescent properties, low toxicity for biological applications, and low cost of synthesis [2–6]. The term CDs indicates the generic name given to describe carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials with sizes on the order of 1–10 nm, these structures are classified according to the specific shape of the carbon core and the chemical groups on the surface, and can appear in the form of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs) or carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) [7,8]. The GQDs are small fragments that consist of one or a few layers of graphene with chemical groups on the edge arranged in specific positions, creating boundary conditions characteristic of zero-dimensional confinement of the π-conjugated domains. CPDs have a hybrid polymer/carbon structure comprising functional groups with abundant chains on the surface and a carbon core. CNDs do not have a well-defined crystalline structure, but show a high degree of carbonization with some functional groups on the surface, in general, the photoluminescence originates in the defect states on the surface and graphitic subdomains in the core, without confinement effects. Finally, CQDs are made up of a core formed by stacking several graphene fragments and a surface rich in carboxylic, hydroxyl, alkyl, and sulfonates, among others. In these structures, there is competition in the luminescence emission created by electronic transitions between conduction and valence bands (band-to-band transitions) and trap emission produced by the chemical groups on the surface [9–12]. In this way, CQDs with large conjugated π domains and few surface functional groups show a dominant fluorescence due to the quantum confinement effect of the conjugated π electrons. Thus, variations in the size of the nanoparticles lead to shifts in the material gap and, therefore, to changes in both the emission and absorption bands of the material. On the other hand, structures with several functional groups on the surface create different energy levels and defect states, whose luminescent emission depends on the excitation energy itself in the material [13–17]. However, recent studies show that CDs synthesized through top-down methods present a relatively low luminescence, being necessary to carry out passivation or surface functionalize in order to increase the quantum yield [17,18]. Furthermore, by controlling the size of the structure, it is not possible, necessarily, to control the wavelength of the light emitted in the photoluminescence measurements [13,19,20]. Therefore, it is understood that the contribution of the core in the CDs emission is negligible when compared to the surface states, mainly associated with the n-□* transition of non-bonding electrons present at the edges of the sp2 domains, the oxygenated functional groups created in the structure surface [21]. Thus, a detailed analysis of the luminescence spectrum becomes necessary to identify the origin of the emission in nanoparticles of CDs.

Despite the photoluminescence technique being a relatively simple and inexpensive technique, the study of the bands obtained in the spectrum makes it possible to identify the nanoparticles’ structural properties, since the optical properties observed in the emission centers and the morphological characteristics of the CDs are closely related in these nanoparticles. In this study, we intend to identify the origin of CDs emission using the photoluminescence and excitation photoluminescence (PLE) technique. The investigated samples of CDs were synthesized from MCC. We used different sulfonation times in the synthesis and performed several rinses of the sulfonated material (to control the pH) in order to evaluate the behavior of the luminescent emission centers as a function of cellulose carbonization and the protonation/deprotonation effects of the functional groups in the surface, respectively. We performed adjustments with symmetric Gaussian deconvolutions in the PL spectrum, we evaluated the dependence of the FWHM adjustment parameters (full width at half maximum) and maximum emission wavelength (λ Max) with the excitation wavelength. With these studies, we intend to show that, from a systematic development of PL adjustments, it is possible to identify the structural characteristics of CDs nanoparticles. In turn, the analyzes developed in this work should contribute to the compression of the mechanisms of luminescence in CDs, which still remain a debate due to the complexity of the surface depending on the synthesis process to which it was submitted.
Microcrystalline Cellulose and Concentrated sulfuric acid used for the production of CDs were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and Êxodo, respectively.

CDs are obtained from the liquid fraction of the production process of a solid carbon-based acid catalyst through the simultaneous carbonization and sulfonation of microcrystalline cellulose (MCC) with concentrated sulfuric acid, as shown in Fig. 1 [22]. The carbonization/sulfonation procedure was carried out at a temperature of 125 °C, for periods of 1 and 6 h, with a H2SO4: MCC ratio of 20:1 (v:w). When the reaction time was reached, ultrapure water was added to the reaction mixture to precipitate the carbon-based acid catalyst, filtered and the solid material was rinsed with ultrapure water. In the step of rinsing the carbonaceous material, two aliquots of the solutions of each product were separated, in which the presence of CDs was identified, being an aliquot of the first rinse, which had a pH equal to 0 due to the concentration of sulfuric acid, and the second aliquot, from the last rinse after acid removal, presented pH 5. The nomenclature to identify the samples in the article is described as t:X_pH: Y, where X is the time in hours during carbonization/sulfonation and Y corresponds to the pH of the solution containing the samples.

For spectroscopic characterization of the liquid fraction obtained from the reaction, 4 mL aliquots of the solution were added to a quartz cuvette with a 1.0 cm optical path for evaluated optical absorption UV–Vis spectra using UV–Vis–NIR Cary 5000 Series, Agilent Technologies spectrophotometer. The spectra were collected in a wavelength range of 600 to 200 nm. Next, PL and PLE measurements were taken using an Agilent Cary Eclipse spectrofluorimeter. For photoluminescence measurements, the samples were excited with light at different wavelengths, between 250 and 400 nm. The PLE measurements were performed by fixing the light detection in each of the emission centers observed in the deconvolution of the luminescence spectra, in these measurements, the excitation wavelength was varied from 250 nm to a wavelength close to the detection. To identify the functional groups of the CDs, the measurements of infrared spectroscopy with Fourier transform with attenuated total reflectance (FTIR-ATR) were evaluated in an Agilent Cary 630 FTIR spectrometer. Finally, the structural analyzes of the materials were performed in a Raman spectrometer. WITec Alpha300 R 128 using an 8 mW green laser at 532 nm to acquire Raman spectra. Finally, to evaluate the structure's information of the carbon dots Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) investigations were carried out.

Scanning electron microscopy SEM measurements were performed for the sample t:1h_pH:5. The figure shows the formation of two types of structures, rigid plates without porosity, agglomerates structures between these plates, and other particles suspended in groups on the rigid flakes. These agglomerates suggest the clustering effect formation of the CDs synthesized as can be seen in Fig. 2
. The presence of elements like C, O, and S in the CDs is confirmed by the energy dispersive spectrometry (EDS) analysis of the Carbon Dots.
Through ATR-FTIR analysis, characteristic infrared spectra were obtained for the material produced. In these measurements, we selected the sample t:1h_pH:5.

The bands around 1030 cm−1-1040 cm−1, can be attributed mainly to the stretching of the SO3H group. It can also be observed the presence of a band at approximately 1625 cm−1, is associated with the C = C stretching of aromatic structures [23,24]. In this way, it is also possible to identify the presence of oxygenated groups in the graphitic core, edge groups such as carbonyls or carboxyls at ∼1700 cm−1, and C–O–C functional groups, around 1120 cm−1. Finally, a band at 3360 cm−1 refers to the – OH, which indicates the presence of carboxylic acid and alcohol functions on the surface of the material.

Raman spectra for two selected carbon dots samples are shown in Fig. 4 . Here, the D and G bands are identified around 1360 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1, respectively. The D band is often associated with the degree of disorder, and structure amorphism, resulting from sp3 carbon bonds, structural defects, or sp2 domain disorder, the G band is associated with graphitic structures, resulting from the vibration of sp2 carbons arranged in a planar shape in the core of CDts. Thus, the ratio of the intensities of the D and G bands (ID/IG) do not present significant differences in both spectra due to the influence of pH, which has been observed in all samples studied in this work around 0.7. The value of the ID/IG ratio being less than 1 [25], indicates that functional groups due to the presence of oxygen have been reduced during the process of obtaining of CDts, these results are consistent with FTIR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescence, photoluminescence excitation (PLE), and absorption characteristics of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 shows the PL and PLE absorption spectra for the samples t:1h_pH:0 and t:6h_pH:0. The UV–vis absorption spectra show maximum absorption in the UV region, which decreases in the visible region. They present several absorption bands due to electronic transitions associated with aromatic species. The bands can be divided into three groups; the (I) band of (220–270) nm can be attributed to the intrinsic (π−π*) transition state of the sp2 aromatic domains (C=C). The band (II) of (280–350) nm originated from the defect states (n−π*) that mainly include oxygenated functional groups CO and C=O [23]. A band at 356 nm is also observed in the sample t:1h_pH5, which we call band (III) and which is generally attributed to transitions (n−π*) of oxygenated aromatic species. Photoluminescent excitation presents two main bands: one around 220* nm and another at 355* nm. These bands combine well with the absorption bands (I) and (II) in the UV–Vis spectrum, indicating that the blue band in the photoluminescence is a result of the contributions that come from the transitions in the CDs core (sp2 structures of graphite) and transitions in the surface defect states (sp³ matrix structures) [11,21].

In Fig. 6 the photoluminescence spectra for the sample t:1h_pH:0 is presented. It is observed that both the shape and spectrum intensity depend on the wavelength used in the excitation (⎣ Exct ). This result in CDs has been widely studied and it is understood that, due to the surface states arising from the functional groups, different trap states are created that create confinements similar to quantum wells of different dimensions, thus resulting in a spectrum of emission that depends on the excitation energy itself used for luminescence. Excitation energies smaller than the gap of the material should induce the emission of the surface states, on the other hand, excitation energies larger than the gap should create an electron-hole pair whose highest probability of recombination is at the edges of the conduction and valence bands (direct band-to-band transition), which in turn, depend on the material core size. As illustrated in Fig. 5, some spectra clearly show the composition of several bands.

To better explore this behavior, we performed Gaussian adjustments with replicas, where the adjustment involving three Gaussians was the one that best simulated each luminescence spectrum in the cumulative curve. We selected a spectrum from each sample studied to present the Gaussian fit with deconvoluted bands. The spectra with their respective adjustments are shown in Fig. 7 . We will label the bands as Band 1, Band 2, and Band 3 centered on increasing wavelengths. In Fig. 7 we show the evolution of luminescence with the pH of the samples and the reaction time. In general, it is observed that band 1 undergoes fewer changes to these two parameters, however, it is observed that for reaction times of 1h the pH produces significant changes only in Band 2. In turn, changes with reaction time are less abrupt for samples with higher pH. These results must be related to the increase in the carbonization degree of the structures according to the reaction time, decreasing the amount of oxygen and, therefore, the functional groups formed on the surface. This should also affect the interior of the structures, making the core of the nanoparticle with higher graphitization. In turn, this effect is further intensified with the protonation effect of carbonyl groups in more acidic samples with pH:0.

In Fig. 8 we analyze the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the three bands obtained in the Gaussian adjustments as a function of ⎣ Exct for all the samples studied. It is observed that band 1 FWHM is smaller for all samples and remains constant with ⎣ Exct. However, the FWHM for bands 2 and 3 show a slight reduction as the ⎣ Exct increases. In CDs, three radiative emission mechanisms are attributed: Emission from the graphitic core in the interior, emission of functional groups linked to the graphitic group known as boundary states, and emission due to the quantum traps existing on the surface. However, associating one of the luminescence spectrum bands to the emission of the nucleus remains a discussion, because top-down methods show that there is only luminescence emission if there is passivation or surface functionalization, in this way, the emission is associated only with the surface states [17]. As the width of band 1 does not depend on ⎣ Exct in the same sample, the emission associated with this band must correspond to fixed electronic transitions [26]. This fact cannot be attributed to the states of traps present on the surface, but to the core of the structures.

From the Gaussian adjustments, it is also possible to extract the emission center of each band. In Fig. 9 we analyze the dependence of the emission wavelength with ⎣ Exct
. According to Fig. 8, we observe that the linewidth of band 1, associated with the core emission, remains relatively constant as the ⎣ Exct increases, indicating the formation of homogeneous cores, but not of the same size, since the emission of the band 1 in Fig. 3 shows that a gradual increment of ⎣ Exct results in a resonance with a CD core of progressively increasing size.

A schematic representation of the excitation of different groups of nanoparticles with increasing sizes and their respective transitions is represented in Fig. 10 . We illustrate families containing nanoparticle clusters with sizes relatively close to each other. Emission of the nanoparticles from each cluster will be allowed if the associated excitation energy is resonant to the band-band transition of the nanoparticles.

To determine the origin of the deconvoluted bands in the photoluminescence spectrum (Fig. 11 ), PLE measurements were performed centered on each of the bands, in specific positions that would prevent their superposition in the absorption states reading.

From the spectrum, it is observed that the origin of absorption of band 1 presents a more homogeneous and thinner PLE band (FHWM ∼ 25 nm), characteristic of homogeneous nuclei, corroborating the results obtained through the Gaussian adjustments [27,28]. On the other hand, bands 2 and 3, associated with defect and surface states, have a line width of approximately 90 nm and irregular PLE spectra, typical behavior of localized states coming from traps. In turn, these bands extend to the band assigned to the core indicating a coupling between the surface states and the core states.

CDs derived from the carbonization and sulfonation of Microcrystalline Cellulose were produced. The deconvolution of the luminescence spectrum showed three electronic transitions. We carried out a study of the dependence of the luminescent emission on the pH of the medium and the time of carbonization of the cellulose. By analyzing the bandwidth and emission wavelength of each of the bands obtained in the deconvoluted spectrum as a function of the excitation wavelength, it was observed that the first state represents the electronic transitions from the conduction band to the valence band (recombination of carriers) within the core of the nanoparticle, the second and third bands were associated with the trap states created at the surface, especially by protonated oxygen groups. This work exposes a methodology to identify morphological features of CDs using photoluminescence measurements, in particular, we identify the formation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The luminescence analysis protocols set forth here can be used to perform other structural evaluations on CDs.

R. F. Cuevas and W. J. F. da Silva Filho: Conceptualization, Investigation, methodology, software, and analysis: Investigation, methodology; R. M. N. de Assunção: Data curation, Formal analysis, Validation; M.C.G. Souza: Data curation, Formal analysis; Ana M.C. Farfan: Data curation, Formal analysis; Validation; M. A. G. Balanta: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the Brazilian financial agencies CNPq, CAPES, FAPESP, and FAPEMIG .M.A.G.B. acknowledges FAPEMIG Grant No. CEX-APQ-00753-18. The authors wish to thank Financiadora de Estudos e Projetos (Grant Numbers 01.11.0135.00 and 01.13.0371.00) and the Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior for scholarship.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Raman spectroscopy for the study of reduction mechanisms and optimization of conductivity in graphene oxide thin films
